speaker: interviewer
text: 1994 Interview with Margot Heuman. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about Auschwitz-- that you were there for six weeks. How did you come to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a transport, and we were selected to leave. They-- they chose people that they thought would be able to work for them, and we didn't know where. Somewhere in Germany. At the time, Dieter told me, don't try to get out of it. Someone-- someone had told her this was a way to get out. We would not be gassed. So I tried to get my mother and my sister to come along. And it was all women, so my father couldn't have come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he stayed at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They wouldn't take my sister. She was too young. So my mother decided to stay with my sister because she felt that Laura needed her more because she was younger, and she also felt that I had Dieter, and I would have someone to be with. So she stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to them there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know what happened to them afterwards. I never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I marched out of Auschwitz with this-- this group of-- we were, again, about 500 young women, and my father was standing in front of his barrack, and I was-- I went over to him, and he blessed me, and that's the first time I ever saw my father cry. And the last thing he said that we should stick together, Dieter and I. That's the last I ever-- I never saw him again. I never heard from him.

words.text: again. I never heard from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skeleton. Very thin, haggard looking. The only thing is my father had stomach ulcers and had problems all his life, until he went to the concentration camp and had no rich food, and he was cured. Really, he had no stomach problems. And my mother had insisted that I take her soup. And I remember when I hugged her goodbye, she was skin and bones, and I ate her soup. And then later on, I had all this food, which I'll tell you later. And-- and I had eaten her soup, and she wouldn't get anything for 24 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother? Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, when I left her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, to leave your mother and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought-- I didn't think I'd never see them again. I thought we'd-- we'd part for a while, and we'd meet again. I never thought about it. I was-- I mean, I was upset, but I was more upset later on when I realized what had happened. But at the time, I just-- I mean, I left saying, well, I'll see you in a year or so. I never thought that they would be killed there-- which was probably naive, but I didn't think of it. And when we marched out of the-- of Birkenau, we went into this other camp in Auschwitz. And at this point, all the people-- I don't know where they got it-- had food! Sweet condensed milk, and sugar, and butter. And everything was going to be taken away from us, so they shared it with us. So I ate more than I had eaten in God knows how long, and here I had eaten my mother's soup. So I just felt terrible about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp was this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Birkenau going into Auschwitz. They kept the-- had the food in Birkenau. I don't know where they got it from. They shared it because they have to give it up. So they gave it to-- we shared-- you know, they gave it to everyone. Then we had to take all our clothes off, and then we were examined by a physician.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman, I remember that. I don't know. A German woman. And then we took showers. But at this point, somehow we were no longer in Birkenau, so we were no longer near there. So we-- we weren't really upset about the showers. They were not mass showers. They were like-- a couple of people together in a stall shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the examinations like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a complete physical, including an internal examination, because people had hidden jewelries and stuff. I-- I-- I don't know. I was scared to death, but I think the doctor finally realized and just left me alone. And then we went outside, and we stood around naked for the rest of the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- it was-- the sun was out. It was cool, but not that bad. Then we were given some soup. And then they took us one by one, and we were outfitted to clothes, for clothes. I was the first-- Dieter stayed behind. I went before her, so I was given this gray and blue striped prisoner's dress, and a kerchief, and some clogs like and a pair of socks. But no underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I got out, she was standing in another area while I was in the new one. I lifted up my dress-- [LAUGHS] I showed her, look, I have a dress, but no underwear. In the meantime, they were still all nude. After a while, you're-- you're completely oblivious to it. Who cares? You know, the SS guys go by-- but you don't even think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then she finally got her clothes. And then we were put into another cattle car, and we were off to Hamburg to work. And we stayed, in the beginning, in the mountains, in barracks. And we worked in quarries. And we used-- Again, we had food, but not enough. We had German soldiers, rather than SS, looking after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you? Who were the other workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly-- I think 95% percent Czech women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all the same-- more or less within 10 years, the same age.

speaker: interviewer
text: No men?

speaker: interviewee
text: No men. Mm mm. And we used to pick mushrooms and make mushroom soup. And we didn't know anything about mushrooms, but we were never poisoned, any of us. And in the winter, we didn't have enough clothing. It was cold. We were always hungry. We had to work hard. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- in the winter, when there was ice and snow, we had no shoes, proper. Our feet-- my feet were frozen. My hands were frozen at one point. But I never gave up hope. But at this point I missed my family, and I realized that I probably-- I didn't know whether I would ever see them again, but I knew that I would not see them in the foreseeable future. So I was very upset about that, and I really-- it just hit home all of a sudden. And besides Dieter-- Dieter had an aunt who was with us. Her name was Liesl, and she was very good to-- to me, too. And she took care of-- like, she took me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 35 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the barracks like that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunk beds. Not too bad. I mean, like a prisoner of war barrack. And that ended, and then they moved us to a place called Freihafen, which is in Hamburg, the city of Hamburg itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was toward the end of '44. And there, we worked as a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] digging traps for tanks. They expected an invasion from England. Also, going into the city and take into bombed-out buildings and cleaning rocks so that they could use them again. Occasionally bombings were started, which made us very happy. Mixed feelings-- happy and-- and also scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, the bombings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to go-- we had to go into a bomb shelter at night. But after about a month, Dieter and I decided, we are not going anymore, this is ridiculous. We had to get up at-- had to leave the house at 5-- the house, the barracks-- at 5:00 AM, because we had to travel on the Elbe, which is the river, by boat, to get to the place that we had to work. And we came home at 6:00 in the evening. And we were utterly exhausted. So we decided we could just-- we're going to hide under the bed, and we were just going to stay. And well, the bombs were-- when you hear them, you're safe. So if it's a direct hit, you don't know the difference. So at that point, we really didn't care anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know that you were hiding under the bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. We just stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you eating when you were in the camp? What kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't have enough to eat. We never had enough to eat. But there were prisoners-- Italian prisoners of war in the barrack next to us. And Dieter made a friend of a young man called Aldo-- I never forgot this-- and they got packages from the Red Cross. And we devised a method where you swing a rope back and forth, and he shared food with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, condensed milk. This really thick, sweet stuff, and dried meat, and things like biscuits. And he just sent them to us. So we shared-- but Dieter and I shared everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Aldo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young. Probably early-- late teens, early twenties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what ever happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Dieter tried to contact him, but we couldn't find him. And there was a-- since we were near the harbor, there were some houseboats that German families who lived there. And there was a German family who left a bowl of porridge outside the houseboat for me every single morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- you were able to leave the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was working on my way to work, and I picked it up. And then I shared it with Dieter, and we left the empty bowl there, and the next day they gave us one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you weren't escorted by--

speaker: interviewee
text: By German soldiers, at that point, and they were not the SS. We had some SS in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the difference between the German soldiers and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: A soldier is a soldier. An SS is a fanatic. I mean, this basically it. And the soldier had to be a soldier. For the SS men, they volunteered. Most of the soldiers were not necessarily anti-Semitic, where the SS-- they are anti-Semites, or they wouldn't have joined the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, was it easier during the labor camp than it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no comparison. Oh, yes. The bombings were bad. We had at least 100 people killed in bombings. We were in bombings in an open field where we had to lie down flat on our stomachs. And Dieter and I were lying next to each other and just holding each other down. And the people that jumped up were just blown to bits. All of us. I mean, the pieces are just flying. Things like that, you can't-- But then we were happy because we thought, gee, the war's going to be over. So it's just a very mixed emotion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever met the people that left you porridge?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just left it every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a German soldier who shared his lunch with me every day because I reminded him of his daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Did you talk to him then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Briefly. So not all Germans were bad. I think the Germans were bad because they didn't object. Silence is guilt, in my opinion. But they weren't all Nazis. Even so, the majority probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never even knew his name. And then we had an SS woman called Bubi. I'll never forget her. I think she was a lesbian. She liked women. She got this one young girl, and all of a sudden, this one young girl had all kinds of privileges. Then, towards the end, she put on a prisoner's uniform and she tried to be one of us, just before we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this SS woman. But no, she wasn't for long, believe me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she treat this young--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what actually happened, but she-- she was-- she-- she was-- I don't know what she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: This woman? Early twenties. Young.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she treat all of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't really abuse anyone, but she wouldn't have had the job if she hadn't volunteered. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who else you were living with, besides Dieter?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three Czech women, and Dieter and I refer to them now-- and a mother. Four-- We refer to them as the Gabor sisters. They were not-- I mean, they were not-- they were three blonde, beautiful women, highly educated, from Prague. I remember them. I don't even remember their names. I just remember them now-- I say the Gabor sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you friends with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only was friends with Dieter. You didn't have many friends under those circumstances. Most people it was dog-eat-dog. And I actually, in all those years, I have never ate anything without sharing it. And neither did she. And we used to pretend-- we used to talk about what it's going to be like when we get out of here. We never, ever gave up hope, and we always-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, we always felt that we made it this far, and we have to make it and get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: All that time, how did the other Jews, and the other prisoners, treat each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some ignored each other. Others stole from each other. Others-- others reported them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never got involved in it. I don't think I ever lost my dignity as a human being. Maybe towards the very end in Bergen-Belsen, because I was very sick. But on the whole-- and I think the difference is, if you care for someone besides yourself. Like they found now that old people who have a pet are better off than old people that are just alone. You can't just be an entity all by yourself. You need something else. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, it's caring and sharing, I think, that makes the difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to celebrate any of the holidays during-- from Auschwitz and the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birthdays.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate birthdays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Dieter remembers that the greatest birthday present she ever got was a half-rotten potato with a face in it that I gave her. I don't even remember it, but she talks about that now.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate birthdays?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. Little-- we couldn't celebrate. We were too tired. I mean, you work-- you're up 12 to 14 hours a day, and work eight to 10 hours at hard, hard labor. And have not enough-- you don't have enough to eat. You're cold. I mean, how--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you keep track of the days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, day and night, but not day, or month or-- well, I guess we heard it from others. But you used to have-- did not-- one day ran into the next. It didn't matter anymore whether it was Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday-- whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never celebrated the sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing at all. No religious--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't even believe in God anymore, at that point. I mean, I just felt that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, If there were a God, this wouldn't be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other people pray or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I know. No one that I was in contact with. Except when there were bombings, and you were really, utterly, utterly helpless-- I think you do have a tendency to pray. But basically , I just felt that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get on with my life-- try to hold on, and hopefully this will be over. Because the bombings increased to the point where they-- I mean, they were constantly exploding, day and night. And the more they were bombing, the happier we got. And we knew that if this continued, it couldn't be too much longer. And all it was just hold out. Hold out. Except the thing that really happened is that we then were-- one day, they told us, pack up. You're leaving. And we were-- went partially by train and partially by foot towards Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where-- did they tell you you were going to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What belongings did you have at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have anything. We had-- we had what we-- the clothes on our back. We had nothing to eat, which didn't matter, because we never-- we didn't have anything to eat anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What clothes did  you have at that point? Still the uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoner's dresses. But we made underwear in the meantime. We had made that ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make underwear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got some material or something, so we made it. Most of us didn't have shoes anymore. We just had rags on our feet. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have rats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rats? Yeah. Lots of rats. Yuck. They used to run around this barrack we were in. Oh, and the barrack had this one wash basin in the front. So, we had to run through the barracks-- we ran in the nude through the barracks and got washed. We always got washed, somehow. So we didn't have any-- any bugs, or anything like that. And just completely oblivious to SS men walking back and forth, and-- didn't bother us. And the only abuse I ever-- I was abused by SS was when I-- if they thought I wasn't fast enough, they'd kick me down the stairs, or things like that. But I have seen them beat up other prisoners. Other women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they kill anybody in front of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just that baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you went on the transport, another train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was partially train, and partially walking. And this was in probably late March of '45. And there was still snow on the ground, because I remember walking in these rags, and all that, and being-- by that point, we were skin and bones. We had no food. We had no clothing. We didn't have nothing anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were sick then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. I didn't get sick until Bergen-Belsen. I was weak, but not sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you have to walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took days. I don't know how much it actually is in kilometers or miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got some soup and some water. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 400 or so. The ones that-- about 100 had died one way or another, or through bombs, or other ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were still the people that came from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were still together, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still with Dieter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm. Oh, yeah. We were together to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you got on a-- on a railroad at some point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Partially. A partial railroad, and then we had to get off again and walk towards-- till the-- till the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: The railroad was a cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then walking into Bergen-Belsen, the first thing I remember about Bergen-Belsen is corpses lined up on both sides of the road, one on top of the other, as high as trees. And the stench, and the prisoners we saw who were there-- people-- the inmates-- concentration camp inmates-- looking worse than we did. And we were bad off. But they were just unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hadn't seen corpses like that before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Nothing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you feeling at that point? Did you know what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think I was thinking, basically, it's now or never. If the war-- war isn't over, we're not gonna last. I mean, you can't last with this. It's impossible. And then we were put into barracks-- and this is where Dieter and I were separated-- and I don't know what happened anymore, because then I got sick, and I was too-- and we were sleeping about eight to 10 people on a bunk, foot, foot, together like this. And every morning, we got up. And we saw who was dead, and took the dead out, and dumped them on top of the pile, and then somebody new came in. And this went on for a month, every single day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever bury people or do  anything religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They just stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to carry the bodies to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We dragged them out. We couldn't carry them. And then we had no food for the last two weeks at all, and no water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six weeks. And-- not even. Probably five weeks. And then we had no water, and I drank water from a puddle-- rain water-- and that's when I got sick. I really got really sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did you have typhoid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't really have typhoid, but I had dysentery so badly that I couldn't-- I couldn't move anymore. I couldn't walk anymore. I was completely dehydrated. And then the-- then we heard the shooting, more, and more, and more. And we-- we knew that the end was in sight, because the SS people were trying to put down their guns. And this woman I told you about, Bubi, put on one of our uniforms, and somebody else did--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Bubi was still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. She was with us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came from the work--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Hamburg, with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- was there any medical treatment at all for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just sick, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. And I still crawled out of bed so every day, because I didn't want to be among the-- the dead to be dumped out on those piles. And then when we-- all of a sudden, we heard this announcement that the Allied forces have entered the concentration camp, and the-- we should hold, and food will be coming, we should hold on. And at that point, I was so sick I didn't care anymore. I just-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Food came. And that make me sicker, because I ate the food that I wasn't used to. And then the medic people came in to take-- to treat all the sick. And then they decided-- since I was a German Jew, they wouldn't take care of me because I was German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- you were liberated by the British?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like when the British first came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I remember is this announcement. And then I-- I understand that most of the soldiers got sick just looking at us, and looking to see what was there. And then they set up these soup kitchens, and bread, and then-- I-- I was too sick. I just-- I couldn't do anything else. I couldn't move anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh huh. Probably the end of April, early May. And then I decided, well, you see, I still had this with me. I said, they don't want to treat me as a German? Then I'll become a Czech. So I went back, and I said, I'm Czech, and they treated me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was that simple?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the British wouldn't treat any German Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So because I was with all these Czech people anyhow. So I said, well, I'll be Czech. So then I remember that I had to leave all my clothes behind. They put me on a stretcher. They carried me, and they had doctors examining me, and they put me into an infirmary, and I was taken care of by French nuns. And I was completely out of it for about a week. And I woke up, and there was little bell, and a cross above my bed, and I just opened my bed, and all I said-- no, no, no, no. I'm Jewish. And I was out again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was unconscious again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where Dieter was during this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She found me in the infirmary, eventually. But I don't know where she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the infirmary for a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in there, altogether, for six weeks before I could even walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was-- the infirmary was set up at--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was set up at the camp by the British and volunteer Red Cross people from all over the world, like the French nuns. Then British soldiers came-- soldiers came came visiting us, and I had had some English in school, so I was able to talk English to them. And they brought us chocolate and things like that we hadn't seen in I don't know how long. At that point, by the way, I weighed 78 pounds. And I also found out that all the food was poisoned, and that the Germans had planned to give all this food to us. The British came too quickly, just before we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? They had--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had poisoned the food in warehouses, and they were going to feed it to us. And then that would have been a quick end, because everybody would have eaten it. But they never got to do it, which was lucky. Either they never got to do it-- maybe they never wanted to do it. You know, sometimes people have a conscience when it comes down to the last-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ever happened that German woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I know that somebody-- she was deported-- somebody informed, and says, listen, it's not what it seems to be. She's so and so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she hid herself as pretending she was a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. A prisoner, right. I think she stood trial, eventually. I don't remember her last name. I just remember that her name was Bubi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do spell Bubi?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know. No. Funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the soldiers-- treated you well?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very, very kind, and very sweet, and very considerate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that the war was over? That the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us that. That that was it. And then I thought immediately, well, now I have to find my family. I never thought they'd be dead. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even then.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But then, I couldn't do anything until I recovered, because I couldn't walk. It took six weeks before I could walk again. And then the Swedish Red Cross had volunteered to take 400 children to Sweden to recuperate from the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with-- you were with hundreds of children, then, in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked me if I wanted to go. Yeah. No, there were adults too, but they chose the children. You had to be under 16, I believe, to go. So they asked me if I wanted to go. And then I decided, well, I have to get my strength back. I'm going to go, and then I can come back and look for everybody else. So I said yes. And about a week later, we went-- I went to Sweden. And this woman, her name is Marta Dandelnell-- was a volunteer for the Swedish Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: M-A-R-- how do you spell her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-R-T-A and D-A-N-D-E-L-N-E-L-L. And she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to quarantine in a small town called Holsbybrunn in Sweden, and we lived in little cottages-- four people together, which was heaven. Believe me. And I met a lot of people from-- not a lot, but several people from Theresienstadt that I had known in Theresienstadt. We ended up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you actually get transported from hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went by boat. We went first by bus to the boat, by boat to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gothenburg, and then from Gothenburg by train to Malmo, and then to Holsbybrunn by bus again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anyone that you were transported with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's take a break now.
